story:
Betty Masters, shaking and tear-streaked, calls Good Mourning Mortuary to report that her boyfriend, Todd Bentley, has died in his sleep. An impossibly soothing AI voice, Arin-Inez, identifies her location by her cell phone’s GPS and offers a “death care unit” in just over an hour, assuring her that final arrangements can be handled at home.

When the unit arrives, there’s no person—only a driverless hearse and a hovering, glass-clear casket that speaks through her phone as Alvin-Ivan. Betty leads the casket upstairs by syncing it to her phone. At Alvin-Ivan’s prompt, she taps “Retrieve Remains.” A hidden vacuum neatly lifts Todd into the casket, flips him onto his back, and seals the lid. Dazed, she continues through a menu: cremation, scattering, yes to a memorial. When she enters Todd’s number, the system mines his records and produces a perfect, data-tailored plan: a short service at Forsythe Pier with a jazz quartet, shared memories, scattering, a monarch butterfly release for his beloved Mercury Monarch, and a reception at his favorite seafood house. A guest list appears too—forty-seven people, complete with contact information.

Two names stop her cold: Clothilde Bentley, wife of the deceased, and Sorrel Bentley, daughter. Betty protests—Todd wasn’t married; they’d been together sixteen years. Calm as ever, Alvin-Ivan notes a “glitch,” then explains that records show an 18-year marriage and an 18-year-old child. To proceed, next-of-kin authorization is required. Texts arrive: the marriage license, Sorrel’s birth certificate, and photos spanning wedding day to graduation. Reality crushes Betty. She hurls her phone to the floor, stomps it, screams, and orders everything canceled. Alvin-Ivan apologizes and explains the services are non-cancellable; Good Mourning has a public duty to secure and care for remains. Betty ends the call; the casket thuds to the floor as its stabilizers cut out. Rage surges. She shoves and kicks the unbreakable shell down the stairs, gouging her walls, while Todd lies serenely within.

An alarm—Bobby McFerrin’s “Don’t Worry, Be Happy”—chirps from Todd’s phone, likely a medication reminder, but she can’t find it. The doorbell rings. Two officers arrive, summoned by the mortuary. Polite but firm, they inform her she’s not next of kin and must release the body. “Dispose of the remains” sounds cruel in her ears, but procedure is procedure. They carry the casket out. The driverless hearse glides after their SUV.

Alone, Betty sifts her years with Todd like flour, hunting for lumps—absences, lies, signs. Nothing. Just smooth batter. Then Alvin-Ivan calls through the wall console in the kitchen: Todd arrived safely at the Ninth and Glendale facility. His wife and daughter are on their way to identify him and finalize arrangements. A bill for services rendered is due upon receipt, along with a list of grief counselors. There’s even a cheery slogan: a good mourning today brings good mornings tomorrow.

Betty snatches up the broken phone from the floor, hurls it into the yard, smashes it with a shovel, and buries the pieces—texts, links, photos, both Todds at once. From inside the house, a faint “Good Morning” from Singin’ in the Rain plays—another alarm from his hidden phone. She wipes her face and listens hard.

“See you in the mourning, Todd,” she says softly. “I’ll see you in the mourning.”
